does: This paper introduces three new multimodal language models—Reka Core, Flash, and Edge—trained from scratch to process and reason across text, images, video, and audio modalities. The authors provide comprehensive technical details on model training and conduct extensive empirical evaluation across multiple benchmarks. Reka Core achieves competitive performance with frontier models like GPT-4V on image question answering tasks such as MMMU and VQAv2, and ranks second in blind human evaluations on multimodal chat, outperforming Claude 3 Opus. The smaller variants, Flash and Edge, deliver state-of-the-art results within their respective compute classes, demonstrating that careful training and architecture design can yield outsized performance gains relative to model size. The paper benchmarks these models on standard text tasks including MMLU and GSM8K, establishing their capabilities across diverse reasoning and knowledge domains.
connects: This work builds on the rapid progress in multimodal foundation models that began with vision-language models like CLIP and evolved into large multimodal models such as GPT-4V, Gemini, and Claude 3. The paper addresses ongoing research into efficient model design and training, extending beyond the text-centric approaches of early large language models toward unified processing of multiple input modalities including newly added video and audio. It connects to prior work on scaling laws and compute-optimal model sizing, which seeks to maximize performance per unit of compute, and engages with comprehensive evaluation methodologies that combine automatic benchmarks with blind human evaluation for robust capability assessment. The integration of video and audio alongside text and images represents an extension of multimodal capabilities that responds to increasing demand for truly universal foundation models.
enables: The availability of multiple model sizes—Core, Flash, and Edge—enables deployment across diverse computational contexts, from resource-constrained edge devices to high-capacity inference servers, supporting broader real-world adoption of multimodal AI. The strong performance of smaller variants suggests future directions for distillation, efficient finetuning, and on-device multimodal reasoning. By demonstrating competitive frontier performance, the work validates architectural and training choices that other researchers can build upon, potentially accelerating the development of more capable multimodal systems. The inclusion of video and audio processing opens pathways for applications requiring temporal reasoning and cross-modal understanding in multimedia content analysis. Blind human evaluation results provide a benchmark for future work to measure progress on genuine user preferences, and the models themselves serve as baselines for further research into alignment, interpretability, and specialized fine-tuning for domain-specific tasks.
